nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef slopes and outer reef walls, typically at depths of 3–30 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small crustaceans and zooplankton. In captivity, generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, enriched frozen brine shrimp, and copepods. Multiple small feedings per day (3–5 times) are commonly advised, especially for newly introduced specimens. A refugium producing live copepods and amphipods can help supplement nutrition.

avoidWith: fish that compete aggressively for food

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe, 3288x2466. Sharp, vibrant male against coral showing all diagnostic features: yellowish-green body, red cheek stripe, and electric-blue fin/tail outlines. Best display pick.
Diego Delso, 6007x4005. Very high-res, crisp clean side profile of a male with red cheek stripe, green flank and blue-edged forked tail. Different pose for variety.
Rickard Zerpe, 2530x1898. Tack-sharp, highly saturated male showing the red cheek bar, yellow-green body and electric-blue fin margins; tail streamer nears frame edge.

summary: The Huchtii Anthias (Pseudanthias huchtii) is a colorful reef fish from the Western Central Pacific, recognized by the male's yellowish-green body, orange cheek markings, and electric-blue fin outlines. It is generally considered reef-safe and tends to thrive when kept in a group with one dominant male and several females.
careNotes:
The Huchtii Anthias is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning all individuals are born female, and the dominant female in a group will naturally transition to male if the existing male is removed or lost. It is commonly recommended to keep a single male with a harem of five or more females, as this arrangement helps distribute aggression; keeping two males together tends to result in intense fighting.

Males grow considerably larger than females — up to around 12 cm compared to approximately 6 cm for females.

This species tends to be semi-aggressive, particularly toward conspecifics (members of the same species) and similarly shaped fish that compete for the same food. It is generally considered reef-safe, with no documented harm to corals or sessile invertebrates (animals such as anemones and feather dusters that attach permanently to the reef).

Multiple small feedings each day — three to five times daily — are commonly advised, as specimens that stop eating can decline quickly. A well-established refugium that produces live copepods and amphipods can provide a beneficial supplemental food source.

In the wild, this species inhabits outer reef slopes and walls across the Western Central Pacific — from the Philippines and Indonesia through Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, and Palau, south to Australia's Great Barrier Reef — at depths of 3 to 30 metres.

Anthias as a group are considered prone to jumping; a tight-fitting lid is strongly advisable.
breedingNotes: The Huchtii Anthias has rarely, if ever, been bred in captivity. As a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite, sex change from female to male can occur in an aquarium setting when the dominant male is removed. Captive breeding would require a mature harem setup and specialized larval-rearing conditions, and no established protocol has been documented.

additional.name: SaltwaterFish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, water parameters, diet, and temperament. Note: this source listed native range as Western Indian Ocean/Red Sea, which contradicts FishBase and ReefApp for this species; that range data was not used.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed native range (Western Central Pacific), adult male size (~12 cm), temperature range (22–28°C), and taxonomy. Family listed as Anthiadidae in current FishBase taxonomy.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm), minimum tank recommendation (400 L / 106 gal), diet, and reef-safe status. Confirmed Western Central Pacific native range.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely if ever bred in captivity. The dominant female transitions to male when the group's male is absent, but no established protocol for inducing or sustaining captive spawning has been documented.
